persons.arguments_against: The strongest case against: the stint name carries only the initial 'C', and the dossier flags the single-initial namesake trap, so a different C. Furness-Smith (a relative, or a successor in the same post) could be the Zanzibar Attorney-General. This fails for several reasons. Attorney-General of Zanzibar is a unique, senior legal office, not a generic junior title that recurs across namesakes; the biography states this exact appointment with a date ('atty.-gen., Zanzibar, Mar., 1936') that immediately precedes the stint's first listing year (1937). The corpus contains only 2 officials with this rare hyphenated surname and a single biography, so the namesake pool is tiny. The stint ends in 1940, exactly when the 1950 biography records him moving on ('atty.-gen., Zanz., 1936; T.T., 1940') — the career shape and the staff-list window agree end-to-end. A successor hypothesis would require another barrister named C. Furness-Smith holding the identical office in the identical years, which the biography already claims for itself; no counter-hypothesis survives.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second contemporaneous barrister named C. Furness-Smith serving in East Africa in the late 1930s, or a staff-list record showing the Zanzibar Attorney-General 1937-1940 with a forename other than Cecil.

notes: The seven OCR variants of the surname (FURNES-, FURNNESS-, FURNESSE-, RNESS-) are clearly the same printed biography; the compiled timeline is internally consistent across all editions. The stint's 1937-1940 listing window matches the biography's Zanzibar tenure exactly, including the 1940 transfer to Tanganyika Territory.
